1: 2 Samuel 22 is identical (with minor textual variants) to Psalm 18, which is titled 'A Psalm of David … on the day when YHWH delivered him from the hand of all his enemies and from the hand of Saul.' Its placement at the end of the Samuel narrative (alongside 23:1-7 and the appendices of chs 21-24) is deliberate: the editors frame David's story with its theological interpretive key. The song is a royal thanksgiving hymn (tôdâ) — the genre in which a king who has been delivered from mortal danger publicly testifies to YHWH's rescue in the presence of the community. In ANE courts, the king's public acknowledgment of divine rescue legitimized his rule by demonstrating that the deity had chosen and vindicated him.
2: The opening cluster of metaphors — 'my rock, my fortress, my deliverer, my God, my shield, the horn of my salvation, my stronghold, my refuge, my savior' — draws on geological and military vocabulary rooted in David's actual fugitive experience in the Judean wilderness. The limestone cliffs and caves of Adullam, En-gedi, Masada, and the Negev highlands are the experiential referent behind the rock/fortress/stronghold language. YHWH is not an abstraction but the one who acted in precisely those physical terrains. This is ANE theology at its most concrete: the deity's protection is expressed in the categories of what survival in the landscape actually required.
3: The piling of epithets for YHWH in v2-3 (seven distinct terms) is characteristic of the royal thanksgiving genre in the ancient Near East. Ugaritic hymns similarly accumulate divine epithets at the opening. But where Baal hymns emphasize the storm-god's power, David's poem centers on relational protection — YHWH is 'my' rock, 'my' fortress. The possessive pronouns are theologically significant: this is not generic divine power but covenantal belonging. The hesed framework (covenant loyalty) underlies every epithet.
4: 'I call upon YHWH, who is worthy to be praised, and I am saved from my enemies.' The logic of petition and rescue is the spine of the entire poem: cry → YHWH hears → YHWH acts → rescue → public praise. This is the thanksgiving psalm structure that the Psalter systematizes. The psalm functions as public testimony in the covenant community — 'I was in danger, I called, YHWH delivered, let me tell you what he did' — which is the prototype of the NT gospel testimony structure.
5: The 'waves/breakers of death' and 'torrents of destruction' that surround the psalmist in vv5-6 use ḥeḇlê māweṯ (cords/waves of death) and naḥălê ḇĕliyyaʿal (torrents of worthlessness/destruction). Bĕliyyaʿal is the term for the underworld forces of chaos in Canaanite cosmology — later personified as Belial in Second Temple literature and the NT (2 Cor 6:15). The psalmist is not using mythology literally but drawing on the existing cosmological vocabulary to describe mortal danger at its most extreme: the forces that belong to the realm of death have surrounded him.
6: 'The cords of Sheol entangled me; the snares of death confronted me.' Sheʾôl in OT cosmology is the shadowy underworld where the dead go — not yet the fully developed afterlife theology of intertestamental Judaism, but the realm of diminished existence, distance from YHWH, and the end of covenant relationship. David's language is hyperbolic and cultic: he was so close to death that Sheol's cords already had him. The deliverance YHWH works is a kind of resurrection from the realm of the dead — a theme Paul and the NT writers will apply to Christ's literal resurrection.
7: 'In my distress I called upon YHWH; to my God I called. From his temple he heard my voice, and my cry came to his ears.' The paradox: YHWH dwells in his heavenly temple yet hears the cry of a fugitive in the Judean wilderness. The temple theology of Israel (and its precursors in the tent/tabernacle) centers on YHWH's accessibility: the sovereign of the cosmos attends to the voice of the endangered. This is the theological claim that distinguishes Israelite religion from ANE royal ideology, where the gods primarily attended to the king in ritual contexts, not to the desperate individual in the field.
8: The theophany of vv8-16 is one of the most elaborate in the Hebrew Bible, drawing directly on Canaanite storm-god imagery while thoroughly demythologizing it. In the Ugaritic Baal texts, Baal is the storm-deity who rides the clouds, hurls lightning bolts, and defeats the sea-chaos monster Yam. 2 Samuel 22 / Psalm 18 takes this same imagery — earthquake, smoke, consuming fire, dark clouds, hailstones, lightning — and attributes it entirely to YHWH's intervention on behalf of his servant. The cosmological battle is relocated: not Baal vs Yam, but YHWH responding to his covenant partner's cry. This is not naive mythology but conscious theological reuse of the available imagery.
9: 'Smoke went up from his nostrils, and devouring fire from his mouth; glowing coals flamed forth from him.' The anthropomorphic language for YHWH's anger — fire and smoke from his nostrils — echoes Sinai (Exod 19:18: the mountain smoked because YHWH descended on it in fire) and the consuming-fire theophany tradition. In Canaanite mythology, the storm deity's weapon is fire. Here fire serves YHWH's covenantal protection of his servant. The imagery will be systematized in later prophetic judgment oracles where YHWH's fire consumes enemies of his people (Isa 66:15-16; Nah 1:6).
10: 'He bowed the heavens and came down; thick darkness was under his feet.' The descent theophany — YHWH 'coming down' — is the standard OT formula for divine intervention in history (Gen 11:5; Exod 3:8; 19:11). The darkness under his feet combines the cloud imagery of Sinai (Exod 19:9: 'I will come to you in a thick cloud') with the royal imagery of the divine throne. In ANE palace iconography, the divine sovereign sits on clouds or is carried by cherubim. The poem democratizes this royal theophany: YHWH descends for a fugitive shepherd-king.
11: 'He rode on a cherub and flew; he was seen on the wings of the wind.' The cherubim as YHWH's throne-chariot (cf. Ezek 1; 10) and the wings-of-wind imagery draw on the same Canaanite tradition of the storm deity riding the clouds (Baal's epithet in Ugaritic: rkb ʿrpt, 'rider of the clouds'). YHWH's exclusive claim to this title is asserted by Ps 68:4 and Deut 33:26. The combat/battle frame is important: YHWH rides out to war, and his war is on behalf of his servant. The divine warrior motif here is the background for Revelation 19's rider on the white horse.
12: 'He made darkness around him his canopy, thick clouds, a gathering of water.' The darkness that surrounds YHWH in theophany is paradoxical — the God who is light (1 John 1:5; Ps 27:1) approaches in thick darkness. This darkness is not malevolent but revelatory concealment: YHWH's full glory cannot be directly witnessed (Exod 33:20-23). The cloud-and-darkness tradition goes back to Sinai and runs through the Tent of Meeting, the Temple (1 Kings 8:12: YHWH said he would dwell in thick darkness), and ultimately to the crucifixion darkness (Matt 27:45) — the final moment of God's veiled self-giving.
13: 'Out of the brightness before him hailstones and coals of fire broke through the clouds.' The storm weapons — hail and lightning/fire — are YHWH's battle arsenal against his enemies. In the Exodus narrative, hail and fire are two of the ten plagues (Exod 9:23-24: fire ran down to the earth mixed with the hail). In the conquest period, YHWH throws down large hailstones on the Amorites (Josh 10:11). The poem assembles this tradition of divine war-weaponry to contextualize David's specific deliverances as part of YHWH's ongoing warfare on behalf of covenant Israel.
14: 'YHWH thundered from heaven, and the Most High uttered his voice.' The thunder as divine speech is a motif shared across ANE cultures: Baal's voice is the thunder in Ugaritic poetry. Here YHWH's thunder-voice precedes his combat intervention. The voice of YHWH (qôl YHWH) is a theological theme throughout the Psalter (Ps 29: seven thunders of the divine voice) and culminates in the divine voice from heaven at Jesus's baptism and transfiguration — the Father's public identification of his Son at the beginning and climax of his ministry.
15: 'And he sent out arrows and scattered them; lightning, and routed them.' YHWH's arrows are the lightning bolts — a standard ANE warrior-deity motif. The Ugaritic Baal wields lightning as his weapon against Mot (death) and the sea. Here the lightning routes David's enemies. The poem makes no distinction between the natural phenomenon (lightning in a storm) and divine agency — the storm itself is YHWH's active combat on behalf of his covenant partner. This integration of natural phenomenon and divine action is characteristic of OT theology: YHWH acts through creation, not apart from it.
16: 'Then the channels of the sea were seen, and the foundations of the world were laid bare, at the rebuke of YHWH, at the blast of the breath of his nostrils.' The cosmic exposure of the sea-floor and earth's foundations echoes the Exodus sea-crossing (Exod 14:21-22: YHWH drove back the sea, and the waters were divided, and Israel walked on dry ground). The same cosmic-level theophany that opened a path for Israel at the Red Sea is invoked for David's personal deliverance. Individual and national salvation are on the same continuum — both are YHWH's sovereign intervention over the forces that threaten covenant life.
17: 'He sent from on high, he took me; he drew me out of many waters.' The 'many waters' (mayim rabbîm) is the OT's standard image for overwhelming chaos and mortal danger (cf. Ps 29:3; 32:6; Isa 43:2). The verb 'drew me out' (yimsĕʾēnî) uses the same root as the name Mōšeh (Moses): 'he who was drawn out of the water.' David's rescue from the many waters recapitulates the Moses-from-the-Nile pattern — both are drawn-out-ones whom YHWH rescues from deadly waters for his redemptive purposes.
18: 'He rescued me from my strong enemy, from those who hated me, for they were too mighty for me.' The theological claim is stark: David survived not because of superior military skill but because the enemy was stronger than David, and YHWH intervened. This is the anti-merit logic that runs through the whole poem: David's survival is entirely attributable to divine election and covenant faithfulness, not human prowess. The psalm's placement after the narrative of David's failures (Uriah, Absalom, census) makes this attribution more striking — the man who needed rescue because of his own sins was also the man YHWH inexplicably continued to deliver.
19: 'They confronted me in the day of my calamity, but YHWH was my support.' The phrase 'day of calamity' (yôm ʾêḏî) is a standard expression for the moment of existential crisis. YHWH as 'support' (mišʿān) — a leaning-post, a staff — is an intimate domestic metaphor inserted into the theophanic cosmic battle frame. YHWH the storm-rider is also YHWH the staff one leans on. The oscillation between cosmic sovereignty and intimate personal support is characteristic of Israel's covenant theology.
20: 'He brought me out into a broad place; he rescued me, because he delighted in me.' The 'broad place' (merḥāḇ) is the spatial metaphor for covenant security — the opposite of the ṣar (narrow/straits) of danger. YHWH delivers into spaciousness. But the rationale — 'because he delighted in me' (yĕḥopĕṣ bî) — is the covenant election language of grace: YHWH's delight in David precedes and grounds the rescue. It is not performance-based. Paul's 'he who did not spare his own Son' (Rom 8:32) is this same logic at its ultimate expression.
21: The righteousness claim of vv21-25 — 'YHWH dealt with me according to my righteousness; according to the cleanness of my hands he recompensed me' — has generated theological difficulty since it seems incompatible with David's actual record (Bathsheba, Uriah, Absalom's rebellion partly caused by David's failures). The key is the covenantal and forensic context: the psalm is a post-battle thanksgiving in which David claims that YHWH vindicated him against his enemies because he (David) remained covenant-faithful relative to those specific enemies — not that he was sinlessly perfect. The righteousness claim is relational and situational, not absolute.
22: 'For I have kept the ways of YHWH and have not wickedly departed from my God.' The claim to have kept YHWH's ways stands in tension with 2 Sam 11-12 until we recognize the psalm's probable context: a thanksgiving for military deliverance, perhaps in the context of wars against Saul or the Philistines or foreign nations, not retrospective assessment of his whole life. Saul, by contrast, repeatedly departed from YHWH's explicit commands (1 Sam 15; 28). Relative to Saul as failed king, David's covenant faithfulness is genuine and is what the poem is asserting.
23: 'For all his rules were before me, and his statutes I did not put away from me.' The claim to have kept YHWH's statutes before him echoes the Deuteronomic ideal of the king who keeps a copy of the Torah and reads it 'all the days of his life' (Deut 17:18-20). David is the embodiment — imperfectly — of this royal ideal. The contrast with the kings who multiplied horses, wives, and gold (Deut 17:16-17 = Solomon, explicitly violated) makes David's relative fidelity more visible in hindsight.
24: 'I was blameless before him, and I kept myself from guilt.' Tāmîm (blameless) is the same word used of Job (1:1) and Noah (Gen 6:9). It does not mean sinless perfection but integrity and wholeness — living aligned with covenant relationship. YHWH's verdict on David 'a man after my own heart' (1 Sam 13:14; Acts 13:22) is the covenant-theology reading of tāmîm: David's fundamental orientation toward YHWH was aligned, even when specific acts were catastrophically wrong and required confession and restoration.
25: 'Therefore YHWH has recompensed me according to my righteousness, according to my cleanness in his sight.' The lex talionis logic of covenant — faithfulness receives blessing, faithlessness receives curse (Deut 28) — is affirmed here but immediately balanced by the surrounding context of the poem, which emphasizes YHWH's grace and delight (v20) as the ground of deliverance. The righteousness-recompense claim is not a works-salvation claim but a covenant-law claim: YHWH is just, and his justice includes rewarding those who are in covenant with him.
26: 'With the merciful you show yourself merciful; with the blameless man you show yourself blameless.' The divine-mirror principle (vv26-28) — YHWH reflects back the posture of the human heart — is one of the most searching theological statements in the Psalter. It is not that YHWH changes his character based on human behavior, but that the same divine action (YHWH's consistent holiness and covenant faithfulness) is experienced very differently depending on whether one approaches in covenant loyalty or in rebellion. The crooked experience YHWH's straightness as crooked because their own crookedness distorts their perception.
27: 'With the pure you show yourself pure, and with the crooked you make yourself seem tortuous.' This divine-mirror principle is cited and applied in Proverbs (Prov 3:34: he mocks mockers but gives grace to the humble), quoted in Jas 4:6 and 1 Pet 5:5 as the frame for NT humility ethics. YHWH's humbling of the proud and lifting of the humble is not a mere principle — it is the structural shape of the cross: Christ humbles himself to the point of death, and therefore God exalts him to the highest place (Phil 2:8-9).
28: 'For you save a humble people, but your eyes are on the haughty to bring them down.' The inversion theology of the poem — humble lifted, haughty brought down — is Hannah's prayer (2 Sam 2:1-10), Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55), and the Sermon on the Mount beatitudes ('the poor in spirit … shall inherit the earth') in embryonic form. The structure of the Davidic narrative — Israel's king as a shepherd rejected by the powerful, elevated by YHWH — is the structural template for the Gospel's king rejected and vindicated.
29: 'For you are my lamp, O YHWH, and my God lightens my darkness.' The lamp metaphor shifts from cosmic theophany to intimate personal provision. The lamp in Israelite domestic life is the guarantee of not stumbling in darkness — the most basic form of guidance and safety. YHWH as lamp is also the royal metaphor: 2 Sam 21:17 uses the lamp idiom for David himself ('you shall not go out with us to battle anymore, lest you quench the lamp of Israel'). The Davidic king is the lamp of Israel, and YHWH is the lamp of the Davidic king — a nested lamp-theology that the NT applies to Christ (John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world').
30: 'For by you I can run against a troop, and by my God I can leap over a wall.' The military-prowess claims of vv30-46 are explicitly attributed to divine empowerment — not David's natural ability but YHWH's equipping. This is the inverse of the Deuteronomic warning against relying on military hardware (horses, chariots, numbers): David's victories come through YHWH's direct empowerment of the king. The theology of weakness-made-strong through divine empowerment (Paul: 'when I am weak, then I am strong,' 2 Cor 12:10) is deeply rooted in this warrior-king tradition.
31: 'This God — his way is perfect; the word of YHWH proves true; he is a shield for all those who take refuge in him.' The theological summary of the entire poem: YHWH's way (darKô tāmîm) is perfect/blameless — the same word used of David in v24, now applied to God. The divine perfection grounds the human possibility of covenant faithfulness. His word (ʾimraṯ YHWH ṣĕrûpâ) is refined/tested — the refining-fire metaphor for tested purity. The word proves true through events: the poem is itself the proof-text that YHWH's word of covenant to David has been kept.
32: 'For who is God, but YHWH? And who is a rock, except our God?' The rhetorical questions challenge the ANE divine plurality: in a world where every nation had its patron deity and the gods competed, the poem asserts YHWH's uniqueness. This monotheistic claim in the context of royal victory is politically charged: David's victories over surrounding nations (chs 8, 10) demonstrate that their patron deities cannot protect them. The rock imagery (used seven times in ch 22) frames YHWH as the most stable, permanent, reliable reality — the opposite of the shifting sands of human fortune.
33: 'This God is my strong refuge and has made my way blameless.' YHWH as māʿôz (fortress/stronghold) recapitulates the opening epithet cluster. The claim that YHWH 'made my way blameless' is important: the poet's blamelessness (v24) is not a self-generated achievement but a gift. YHWH enables the covenant faithfulness that the psalm then claims — a grace-enabled righteousness, not a merit-righteousness. This is the same structure as Paul's 'work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you both to will and to work' (Phil 2:12-13).
34: 'He made my feet like the feet of a deer and set me secure on the heights.' The deer-footed image — sure-footedness on mountain terrain — draws on the actual geography of David's military campaigns in the hill country of Judah and the eastern highlands. The feet of a deer (ʾayyālôt, female deer/doe) on rugged mountain terrain is the embodiment of confident agility in dangerous landscape. Habakkuk 3:19 uses the same image: 'God, the Lord, is my strength; he makes my feet like the deer's; he makes me tread on my high places.'
35: 'He trains my hands for war, so that my arms can bend a bow of bronze.' The bow of bronze is the elite weapon of the period — a composite bow reinforced with metal for maximum draw weight and range. In the Iron Age Levant, the ability to draw a bronze-reinforced bow was a mark of elite warrior status. YHWH is credited with the physical training that produces this capacity. The theology is thoroughgoing: natural ability, physical training, and military success are all attributed to YHWH's equipping. There is no secular-sacred division in the poem's worldview.
36: 'You have given me the shield of your salvation, and your gentleness made me great.' The word for gentleness here — ʿanwâ (humility, meekness) — is unexpected in a victory hymn. YHWH's greatness-making is achieved not through overwhelming force but through divine meekness/condescension. This is one of the most theologically loaded words in the psalm: the sovereign of the cosmos stoops down to make a fugitive shepherd-king great. It is the incarnational principle avant la lettre — the divine condescension that makes human greatness possible.
37: 'You gave a wide place for my steps under me, and my feet did not slip.' The spacious-place (rāḥaḇ) imagery recapitulates v20. The stability of David's footsteps is the covenant security metaphor: YHWH secures the path so that the servant does not slip. Slipping (māʿaḏ) is used in the Psalter for covenant failure and the spiritual instability that precedes it (Ps 73:2: 'my feet had almost stumbled; my steps had nearly slipped'). The stability YHWH provides is both physical (in battle) and covenantal (in faithfulness).
38: 'I pursued my enemies and destroyed them, and did not turn back until they were consumed.' The relentless pursuit and destruction of enemies is the ANE royal military formula — the language of the victory stele, the war annals of Egyptian and Assyrian kings. David adopts this genre entirely, but attributes the success to YHWH rather than to his own heroic prowess. This is the standard Hebrew war theology: the verb forms describe David's actions, but v33-36 have already established that every capacity was divinely given. The warrior acts; the victory belongs to YHWH.
39: 'I consumed them; I thrust them through, so that they did not rise; they fell under my feet.' The complete subjugation language — enemies under the feet — is the standard ANE iconography of royal victory: the king placing his foot on the neck of the prostrate enemy (cf. Josh 10:24). In the royal theology of the Psalter, this image becomes the messianic template: 'Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool' (Ps 110:1), cited more often in the NT than any other OT text as the primary lens for understanding Christ's exaltation and cosmic rule.
40: 'For you equipped me with strength for the battle; you made those who rise against me sink under me.' The divine equipping (tĕʾazĕrēnî) and the making-to-sink (wayyikrĕʿû) are both divine acts — the same pattern as Exodus: YHWH fights while Israel advances. The poem's theology of holy war is not that Israel fights for God but that God fights through Israel's obedient participation. When this equation breaks down (Achan at Ai; Saul at Amalek), defeat follows. David's victories confirm that the equation held — provisionally.
41: 'You made my enemies turn their backs to me, those who hated me, and I destroyed them.' Turning the back is the shame-posture of the defeated in ANE warfare — to show one's back is to concede. The enemies' backs turned to David is the visual opposite of the enthroned king before whom conquered peoples prostrate themselves. The theological claim is that YHWH actively turned the enemies, not merely that David defeated them. The verb causative (wayyitēn) marks the action as divine, not human.
42: 'They looked, but there was none to save; they cried to YHWH, but he did not answer them.' The enemies' unanswered cry to YHWH is a pointed inversion: the same cry David makes in v7 (and YHWH answers) the enemies also make in extremity, and YHWH is silent. The contrast maps onto the covenant distinction: YHWH hears those in covenant relationship with him; those who are his enemies in their conduct of life find that the same God who answered David does not answer them. This is not arbitrary — it reflects the covenant structure of the divine-human relationship.
43: 'I beat them fine as the dust of the earth; I crushed them and stamped them down like the mire of the streets.' The crushing and stamping imagery is hyperbolic conquest language — the ANE war report idiom. Comparing defeated enemies to dust and street-mire reduces them to the lowest possible status in a culture where honor and shame were the primary social currency. The theological frame (this is YHWH's victory through David) prevents this from being mere triumphalism — it is the assertion that YHWH's enemies are ultimately brought to nothing.
44: 'You delivered me from strife with my people; you kept me as the head of the nations; people whom I had not known served me.' The shift from foreign enemies to 'strife with my people' acknowledges the civil war with Saul's faction and later Absalom's rebellion. YHWH's preservation of David even through internal Israelite conflict is the most difficult deliverance — external enemies fight at the borders, but internal enemies threaten the covenant community itself. The nations serving David echoes the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3) and the Davidic covenant's expansion to include international rule.
45: 'Foreigners came cringing to me; as soon as they heard of me, they obeyed me.' The tribute-bringing imagery and the voluntary submission of foreign nations to the Davidic king reflects the historical reality of David's imperial expansion (2 Sam 8: subjugation of Moab, Edom, Philistines, Ammonites, Arameans) and the ANE court protocol of subject peoples bringing tribute to the sovereign. In the royal theology, this submission prefigures the eschatological gathering of nations to the Davidic throne — a theme Isaiah (11:10; 42:6; 49:6) develops into the mission of the Servant to the nations.
46: 'Foreigners lost heart and came trembling out of their fortresses.' The metaphor of coming trembling from fortresses — the strongest defensive position of the ancient world — captures the psalmist's point: even the most fortified position is no protection against YHWH's intervention on behalf of his king. The fortress imagery (YHWH as fortress in v2; enemies leaving their fortresses in submission in v46) forms a chiastic frame: the poem begins with the divine fortress protecting David and ends with human fortresses providing no protection for his enemies.
47: 'YHWH lives! And blessed be my rock, and exalted be my God, the rock of my salvation.' The climactic doxology begins with ḥay YHWH — YHWH lives, is living, is the living God (as opposed to the silent, dead idols who could not answer their worshipers in v42). This is the confession of the resurrection life of God — the Psalter's equivalent of the NT's confession that the Father raised Jesus from the dead. The God who acts in history is the living God, not the static divine principle of Greek philosophy.
48: 'The God who gave me vengeance and brought down peoples under me.' Niqmôṯ (vengeance/vindication) in OT theology is not primarily retributive emotion but covenant justice — YHWH's setting-right of covenant wrongs. When YHWH takes vengeance, he is acting as the covenant enforcer who holds people accountable for their treatment of his covenant partners. Deut 32:35 ('vengeance is mine') and Rom 12:19 (Paul quoting it) both insist that YHWH's vengeance is precisely what removes the need for human retaliation — he will act, so we need not.
49: 'Who rescued me from my enemies; yes, you exalted me above those who rose against me; you delivered me from the man of violence.' The triple statement of rescue (enemies, those rising against, violent man) covers the spectrum of David's actual threats: Saul, Absalom, Sheba, foreign nations. The poem is deliberately comprehensive in its scope — YHWH's rescue spans the entire narrative arc of David's life as told in 1-2 Samuel. The placement of this psalm at the narrative's conclusion invites the reader to reread the entire David story through this interpretive lens.
50: 'For this I will praise you, YHWH, among the nations, and sing to your name.' This verse is cited verbatim in Romans 15:9 — Paul's catena of OT texts proving that the Gentile mission was always YHWH's plan. 'I will praise you among the nations' places the Davidic king as the one through whose worship YHWH is glorified among non-Israelite peoples. Paul applies this to Christ: the son of David's praise among the nations is fulfilled in the proclamation of the gospel to all peoples, and Christ himself as the ultimate Davidic king leads this praise (Heb 2:12).
51: 'Great salvation he brings to his king, and shows steadfast love to his anointed, to David and his offspring forever.' The closing verse of 2 Sam 22 / Ps 18 is the most concentrated theological statement in the book: māšîaḥô — his anointed one, his Messiah. The poem is the Messiah's own song, the covenant song of the anointed king. The ḥesed to David and his offspring forever is the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7) in its most compressed form: the eternal covenant-loyalty (ḥesed) runs not just to David but to his seed — and in NT theology, that seed is Christ (Gal 3:16 applies the same seed-logic to the Abrahamic promise: 'to your offspring,' which is Christ).
